item.title: Greetings from Vesuvius
item.content:encoded:
As someone who sees Mt Vesuvius everyday, I find this particularly amusing. And creepy!
+1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first one comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons, and the second image comes from here and is All Rights Reserved. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only one thing I find weird.
In the description it describes the transmutation into igneous rock, and in the addendum it describes the creation of more scp-1120 instances. However in the description, it never states that this transmutation results in the creation of more instances. Maybe it's just me being picky, but it wasn't quite clear on my first reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I very much enjoy this SCiP, combining it with the comment section is the real gem. I can't remember better example of documented step by step organic cultivation. The reasoning behind suggested changes here is sound, the counter arguments are adult and reasoned, the resulting adopted changes have well rounded this article. Future critequers take note, this is how to help an author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Crop dusting is done much, much lower down than where supposed chemtrails are done, though (assuming that "chemtrails" are done at the altitudes that real contrails form at). It's done only a couple hundred feet in the air at most, as opposed to tens of thousands.

A crop-dusting type dispersion would be totally kosher for me. Much less chance to disperse and/or blow downwind. Not sure how one would word it to be more specific than "airborne amnestics" if using that method besides maybe using a "lost crop duster" as a cover.

item.title: Breaks Character
item.content:encoded:
Like others here, I feel the direct reference to the foundation at the end is a bit disconcerting and strains credulity a bit. That being said, the final statement tying into Vesuvius is a very nice closer.

Although it looks like you edited out some references to it, I don't see the nature of bone immunity to the stone growth being explored adequately:while the containment protocol is neat, it doesn't appear to factor in anywhere else. I don't really see it adding anything particularly powerful to the narrative in it's current setting.

No Vote until the direct foundation reference at the end is modified for less mood whiplash.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted because I'm a sucker for anything related to Pompeii and Mt. Vesuvius. Perhaps I'm biased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know about you guys, but I'm actually really liking this one. It's imaginative and makes good use of the pictures. I'm assuming that you found the pictures first and based the skip off of them, which is always good. Anyway, it's a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the bit about someone somehow causing an eruption. That's drastic, creepy and very fitting in the SCP universe where so many of these objects have been lying around since forevernever BC. That there was a once upon a time group that collected them is neat. That the Foundation would be all like "Oh, hey! They had magic! That means they're just like us!" is an odd bit of huberis. That this club just happened to have the same initials in Latin as Special Containment Proceedures has in English is just cartoony and goofy.
The rock monsters… they're okay. They're good all purpose monsters.
I say ditch the pun on "SCP" because it's not working and do more to flesh out the wizard club that made the ultimate sacrifice because it's working quite nicely. No vote. I'll check back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Containment Procedures:

I'm fairly certain you mean "Special Containment Procedures".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I agree that "chemtrails" would be better off as "airborne amnestics" I am not convinced that they would be ineffective. I imagined it being a bit like crop-dusting. They fly over the town, dust it with amnestics and everyone forgets about the crazy stuff that recently messed up the town. I think that since there are a bunch of people today who make a good living crop-dusting, it would not be unimaginable for the Foundation to do something similar. While meteorology is not an exact science, we know it well enough to spread some amnestics without too much weather-related difficulty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it in the chat and I like it here. You have my upvote.

item.title: Re: diamond-tipped
item.content:encoded: Imagine the damage Diamond-Tipped bullets would do though if they stayed together…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, the tie-in with Pompeii seems silly, especially when it sounds like the Foundation knew some sort of wizardry to cause eruption. The final note doesn't even serve to close the article, it just shows how the anomaly is dangerous and couldn't be contained, when its danger has already been reiterated in the Discovery section.

The concept is ok, but personally I think it needs a stronger background.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
May be a bit nit-picky, but:

Could the "chemtrails" bit possibly be changed to a more generic (but still very similar) "airborne amnestics," which also has numerous precedents?
At the height that chemcontrails form, there is little chance that the area below would actually see much effect. Anything sprayed from that height would dissipate and blow away, so you might make a few people several hundred miles downwind forget what they had for breakfast, but your targets would continue remembering (and freaking out about) the stone men they saw. Trying to target it by spraying it upwind would be…difficult, at best. (Though I could be mistaken on all that, as I am not a meteorologist.)
Also, on a more personal-opinion note, the word "chemtrails" makes me want to go find someone with a tinaluminum foil hat and punch them in the face, because supposed "real" chemtrails aren't a thing, unless the chemical in question is water.

The only other thing I have is just a reiteration of the Sumus Custodes Pacis bit. Tacky, as Ihpkmn put it, is a good word for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this, both as a standalone SCP and a possible explanation for Vesuvius. The primitive foundation at the end was a nice touch, except for the point Ihpkmn brought up. The imagery you put forth when describing the… rockification, was really great to imagine. I definitely would give this an upvote, even with some rocky edges.

get it i made a funny

EDIT: I just remembered that I'm going to see the touring Pompeii exhibit in about two weeks. I'm not going to be able to think about anything but this while there.

item.title: Re: diamond-tipped
item.content:encoded:
Actually a shattering effect might be beneficial in terms of damage to the target. A coworker of mine in the security industry uses frangible ammunition specifically for that purpose, since it shatters and transfers its energy so efficiently to the target (not entirely dissimilar to the effect of a shotgun blast).

That being said, diamond really wouldn't add any appreciable benefits to a round, as far as I know. Also, given the difficulty involved in cutting diamond into an exact shape, its utility is further diminished. Possibly to the point of being actively detrimental to the ballistics of the round.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This all looked okay (even if expunging the dig sites is rather baffling, due to the fact that it's obviously near Mount Vesuvius), but then we get to the last note:

It is believed that it was left by an agent of a primitive group functioning as a precursor to the current Foundation

Sumus Custodes Pacis

That… no. Just no. I'm fine with Foundation-like organizations existing in ancient times, but to out right say that the SCP Foundation was a thing in Rome is just tacky.

And before anyone gets on my case: I know it means "We are the peace-keepers", not "Secure Contain Protect". The fact that it's left in Latin so it can be an SCP Acronym betrays its intent, however.

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He was talking about the

~Schrödinger's_Kitty

Which is different from the set wikidot signature. Wikidot signatures are set not to display on this site- what Chork meant is "don't type your name at the end of your posts".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know, but I have my own website and I use a signature there, not for here. Don't know how to fix that so it's only on my website.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess my thought with that was that, if there was an ancient Foundation (or something similar), they could have used some kind of anomaly to trigger the volcano in order to keep these guys contained. I think I was trying to insinuate that with the Greek Fire bit (maybe it was anomalous too?), but I communicated it rather poorly. It's less "one guy holding out against the monsters" and more "the Foundation did this in order to stop things from getting worse". But I understand if it still doesn't work. Thanks for the feedback regardless!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A solid idea. I think there is a little too much information, which detracts from the reader's vision. I'll gladly upvote this if there is some refinement, but for now, keeping neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, I dont know if this is my place to say this, but I don't think you should use a signature. I've heard somewhere that they are generally not necessary or tolerated on the site. Take this with a grain of salt, tho, because I have no authority or understanding over this issue.

But I do agree with what you mentioned above, and don't really understand why this article is in the negatives

item.title: More Cleanup
item.content:encoded: Edited again. Removed the Greek Fire stuff in favor of something more generic.

item.title: Re: diamond-tipped
item.content:encoded:
They also have cleavage planes and will shatter.

Sectional density and toughness matter as much as hardness when it comes to projectiles penetrating other things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said in the forums, the last addendum really kills it for me.

+ Volcano science...
- ...And stuff

The eruption from Vesuvius that destroyed Herculaneum and Pompeii was an explosive one, meaning that there would've had to be a buildup of massive amounts of pressure over time in order to achieve that kind of eruption. Sorry, but a bit of Greek fire thrown into a caldera wouldn't be able to trigger such an event. There were also large quantities of seismic activity leading up to the eruption, and you completely left that bit out.

Also, Greek fire wasn't invented until about 600 years after Vesuvius erupted.

I love volcano-related stuff, and I really wanted to upvote this, but until you can fix the aforementioned problems, I'm novoting.

EDIT: The addendum still just doesn't work. One person simply can't trigger a volcanic eruption. I'm not even sure that the citizens of Pompeii were aware that Vesuvius was an active volcano, and even if they did, the wouldn't know what could make it erupt. I'm still going to novote, but I'm leaning more towards a downvote the more I think about it.

item.title: Re: Cleanup
item.content:encoded: not being able to interact/damage human bone seems sufficient.. and its not like we have a shortage of human bones laying around… (dark headcanon of the details of "termination")

item.title: Re: diamond-tipped
item.content:encoded: uncut diamonds are actually really cheap, and who say we cant use cubic zirconium?

item.title: Cleanup
item.content:encoded:
I've made a few minor tweaks to wording and eliminated the diamond stuff. The intent with that was mainly to give it a vulnerability in order to keep these guys from being too overpowered, but I guess given the cost of the materials needed it didn't make much difference. I'm not sure what I can do to fix the execution without a complete overhaul, so I think for the time being I'll clean it up best I can and see how long it lasts.

Thanks for all the feedback, everyone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh yeah, for some reason I was typing the word "tip" and everything, but I was typing about a diamond-jacket - awkward. I do agree with you that high caliber weaponry would be the way to go, since a diamond tipped bullet probably isn't what the author wants in this scenario.

Thanks for correcting me!

item.title: Re: diamond-tipped
item.content:encoded:
(just two orders of magnitude of the cost of conventional ammunition).

Twelve times that on each magazine. Not beyond imagination, not for the Foundation, but seriously inefficient nonetheless.

like if you were fighting a werewolf made of buckminsterfullerene who had diamond as his silver.

And I wanna read this now. Damn you, Patrick.

item.title: Re: diamond-tipped
item.content:encoded:
would cause extensive damage to any gun using them in a short period of time.

Why? I feel like you are confusing jacketing with tipping, tipped materials should never recontact any internal surface of a gun after chambering, diamond tipping a bullet would seem irrelevant to the life cycle of the internal parts (I'm not firearms expert, so I might be wrong, correct me if that's the case)

I would expect the performance of a diamond tip bullet to be near-enough-as-to-not-matter to any other conventional ammo, since it is just a hollow point with diamond instead of the hollow (more aerodynamic but no mushrooming which was kind of the point of having the hollow in the first place)

I mean ya, its stupid because it is expensive, but making a synthetic diamond tip in volume probably costs in the dozens of dollars range, so it wouldn't be PROHIBITIVELY expensive (just two orders of magnitude of the cost of conventional ammunition).

I think the line should just be use high caliber weaponary though, but it isn't like diamond tipped bullets would be prohibitted from consideration were there a situation which somehow warranted their usage, like if you were fighting a werewolf made of buckminsterfullerene who had diamond as his silver.

item.title: diamond-tipped
item.content:encoded:
I mentioned it in my post but no, diamond tipped bullets are far from practical. For one, they'd be very expensive, and would cause extensive damage to any gun using them in a short period of time. Even though a diamond tipped bullet is hard, it'd either shatter at contact, or just bounce off the intended target due to diamond's low density.

I guess the use of diamond in cutting tools misled the author. Diamond dust leftover from diamond cutting is used on diamond-tipped saws, since the cutting tool only needs hardness, not density.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
++ Reach here!
-- Click to un-Reach!

issued a sufficient number of diamond-tipped bullets.

"… and, at that point, supervisor [REDACTED] simply dropped the budget summary and said 'no, fuck this, we're using plastic explosives.'"

"Man, I love that guy."

Also, completely ignorant question; would diamond-tipped bullets practical at all?

the only known method of penetrating SCP-1120's exterior.

I know rock is hard. I do. But I believe it's not that hard. ,,Also, a diamond-tipped bullet simply has no stopping power. These things can go through walls, why would a single bullet cause enough damage to render them inactive?

Upon entering an active state, an instance of SCP-1120 will patrol the surrounding area aInstances of SCP-1120 exhibit inhuman strength, and have been observed to break through cinderblock walls and destroy sizable pieces of urban infrastructure while in pursuit.

… how do we know it will, if we have only witnessed once since containment?

Instances of SCP-1120 exhibit inhuman strength, and have been observed to break through cinderblock walls and destroy sizable pieces of urban infrastructure while in pursuit.

… eh. Humanoid what is strong is not necessarily bad, but I'm reading this and I can't stop thinking of undead trolls with a preference for construction materials.

Also, wholesale urban infrastructure seems to be part of what they do, which I believe should not be. Unless they hate urban facilities, or are secretly in league with construction contractors.

From this point on, SCP-1120-1 is to be treated as a new instance of SCP-1120 and contained accordingly.

… this belong on the SCP section. Furthermore, call me unimaginative, but I am not really feeling the horror of being turned to stone by reading this paragraph.

though the actual number may be much higher.

If we know the number of instances discovered, we know how many people died and disappeared under the assumed rubble.

To anyone who reads this, get out while you can. I go now to cast the last of the Greek Fire into the heart of Vesuvius.

… that's not even remotely what Greek Fire does, in the real world anyways… but alright.

VERDICT: Reluctant downvote.

See, I don't really mind most parts here. Its technical language is precise and adequate, it's mostly spot on at this incarnation… but it doesn't make me feel anything until the paragraph before the Addendum (which I like probably more than the Addendum itself), and the Addendum itself, which is presented barely without a frame for me to latch onto.

I don't dislike the final Addendum. It's simply failing to impress me (which is my fault, or rather, not yours). Furthermore, the SCP line drop at the end is just not engaging for me. I'd have bought a short explanation on how this was a small group that performed the functions of the Foundation and later became the precursor Foundation, but there is no explanation or expansion on that concept, and thus it falls completely short of interesting.

Finally, we already have anomalous humanoids that turn you into ONE OF US (from 008 upwards, and 008 IS ZOMBIES, so imagine how many permutations have we attempted here). The kick and conclusion is that this item does a lot of things nicely, but I don't see it staying on the mainlist without being a) insanely well-written and b) a lot more interesting.

That said, please keep writing for this site. You've got the tech-speech down to a T, and this counts for a LOT in this format writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few comments:

+nitpicks
– hide block

Diamond-tips doesn't translate very well from tools like saws to bullets. To begin with, it'd wreck your gun. More importantly, it wouldn't be as effective as metals currently used for armor-piercing , since diamond is not dense even though it is hard. I'd suggest replacing the diamond tipped bullets with tungsten tipped bullets.

More of an opinion, but I cannot imagine a spec-ops outfit that would willingly call themselves virgins.

At the end, are you implying Sumus is using Greek fire to force Mt Vesuvius to erupt? If so, there are two scientific and historical reasons that make the idea silly. For one, the known components and effects of Greek fire would not cause a volcano to erupt. Secondly, Greek fire was created and used by the Byzantines, hundreds of years after the eruption in Pompeii. If there is a historian in the forums, feel free to fact check me on this.

I feel the main hook of this article is its historical basis, but the details of Pompeii just feel tacked on to me, and the actual skip is just rock-covered skeletons creating more rock-covered skeletons. The idea has potential, but the execution seems rather bland.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This seems like a good idea. I like how it is based off a real event (the Pompeii eruption) and it is a creepy concept to think about. I like how you included a message on the walls describing their fate. It makes it all the more interesting that I am currently studying Latin. I suggest that they (since there are clearly more of this specific SCP) are intelligent and able to speak. It would make for a very interesting interview. I would like to see more work on this.

~Schrödinger's_Kitty

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Operatives are advised to be watchful and to exercise great caution.

You could (You don't need to) change it to this:

"All Operatives within a suspected zone should remain watchful and exercise caution."

Otherwise, It is a + from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept of this skip is pretty decent. However, there are a few things that I see that could use some fixing.

+ Incoming
- Poof

Starting off, it says in the containment procedure (talking about the discovery of new instances) to see the discovery log for related incidents. First off, it isn't formatted in a log template. You can either change the "discovery log" at the end to incident reports, or even change it into an actual log.

Additionally, it never states what the personnel should do in the event that 1120 becomes active. Given that later in the article you find out that diamond-tipped tools can pierce the rock, shouldn't there be more than just saying how many personnel should be armed with guns with diamond bullets? In the containment procedures, it says nothing about any "active" state. If I were in charge of watching some rock people, I would want to know what I should do if one of them starts moving. Perhaps just expand on what procedures the guards should follow (it could even be "shoot them," so long as there's something like that included.

Then it comes to the walls themselves. You state that instances are capable of destroying walls, so what does bone-marrow concrete add in security? Unless humans have developed titanium bones recently, I don't see how a wall like that would stop it. Perhaps add a clause somewhere that states (or even implies) that they have an aversion to bones and rocks, perhaps saying that they think the walls are their own people, and so won't destroy it.

In the decryption, the line "at the current time, all instances of SCP-1120 are in a dormant state." It should read "current time of writing." Why? Because, as it currently stands, it gives no reference to time. It implies that the instances are always dormant. Continuing in the path of dormancy, what makes them dormant? Does them touching a person make them dormant? Or do they continue attacking people, even after they "tag" a person? As for the new instances generated, are they dormant or active?

Lastly, the addendum. This is just for aesthetics. I've seen some documents that don't label the addendum, and I've seen some that have. My opinion is that it makes the document look more professional when it is labeled. Such as Addendum 1120-a, but it could be 1120-b or 1120-1, however you feel to classify it. Then, there's the set of italics set within the addendum. You could keep it there, or you could move it outside the body and have it where that block is entirely the document itself, and no notes attached.

The overall content is decent, but I thin I'll keep it at a no-vote for now.
